source.title: Contention Scheduling and the Control of Routine Activities

confidence_rationale: The Cooper-Shallice contention scheduling model is the cognitive-neuroscience tradition's canonical formal account of *schemas* competing for execution -- a parallel ancestor lineage to the AI options framework, with the same essential structure (action-schemas as competing units, with activation, gating, and termination), but anchored in patient data (action-disorganisation syndrome). Mapping fidelity to ARC-062 and MECH-312 is HIGH but the inheritance is mixed because the model is largely descriptive rather than mechanistic-quantitative. Confidence not higher because the model has not had the same algorithmic continuation as the options/HRL line; it remains close to its 2000 form.
failure_signatures: Action-disorganisation-syndrome lesion patterns are reproduced in the Cooper-Shallice model by selective lesioning of activation pathways. If REE's MECH-312 arbitration cannot reproduce analogous failure modes (sequence errors, perseveration, capture errors) under selective ablation, the schema/contention-scheduling reading of MECH-312 is weakened.

The schema tradition predicts a Supervisory Attentional System (SAS) that overrides contention scheduling for novel/non-routine situations -- this is a candidate framing for ARC-062's gated_policy. If REE has no SAS-like override and the gated_policy is fully integrated into the same arbitration substrate, that is a divergence.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Routine action selection is governed by competitive activation within a hierarchically organised network of action schemas. Each schema has an activation level, an initiation condition (objects in the environment that excite it), and a termination condition (goal completion). Schemas compete via lateral inhibition; the winner gates motor output. Action-disorganisation syndrome is reproduced in the model by selective lesioning, with characteristic sequencing errors emerging from the lesioned dynamics.
mapping.ree_translation: The contention-scheduling tradition is a parallel ancestor of the AI options framework, with the same core primitives in different vocabulary: schema = option, schema activation = option-policy-value, lateral inhibition between schemas = soft policy-over-options, schema termination = option termination function. ARC-062's gated_policy + discriminator can be re-stated as contention-scheduling between schemas. MECH-312's arbitration is the contention-scheduling competition itself. Importantly, the schema tradition has a distinct primitive that the options tradition does not: the Supervisory Attentional System (SAS, Norman-Shallice 1986), an explicit override mechanism for novel / non-routine situations -- which has a possible mapping to ARC-062's discriminator distinguishing rule-applies vs rule-does-not-apply contexts.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The Cooper-Shallice model is largely descriptive: it reproduces patient lesion patterns in simulation but does not have the algorithmic / convergence-theorem framework of options/HRL. So inheriting from this line gives REE *concepts* (schema, lateral inhibition, SAS) but does not give REE off-the-shelf *algorithms* in the way Bacon 2017 or Eysenbach 2018 do. The two traditions are best read in parallel: vocabulary inheritance from the schema tradition, algorithmic inheritance from the options/HRL tradition.
mapping.source_context: Cognitive neuropsychology paper combining computational simulation (PDP-style schema network) with patient data (action-disorganisation-syndrome from frontal-lobe lesions). Tasks are everyday activities (preparing coffee, lunch-box packing) decomposed into schemas.

confidence_components.notes: Top-tier cognitive-neuropsychology venue, well-cited, but the field has fragmented since 2000 and the model has not had the same algorithmic continuation as the options/HRL line. Mapping fidelity is high for the *concept-level* translation (schema = option). Transfer risk is moderate due to descriptive rather than mechanistic-quantitative nature.
